query: Controlled vs uncontrolled React components interview question

competitors.label: <input> – React
competitors.selectionReason: The official input docs are the strongest canonical source for value/defaultValue, controlled/uncontrolled warnings, and caret-jump pitfalls.
competitors.winReason: The official input docs remain the canonical API reference, but our page is now stronger as an interview answer on ownership, uncontrolled-to-controlled warnings, defaultValue versus value, and the file-input exception that usually appears as the follow-up.
theirStrengths: It is the canonical reference for controlled inputs, defaultValue, and switching-mode warnings.
theirGaps: It is input-focused rather than framed as an interview comparison page.

realWorldUseCases: The production bugs appear when one field tries to switch owners halfway through its lifecycle.
followUpConfusion: defaultValue sets the initial DOM value for uncontrolled inputs; value makes React the owner on every render.
File inputs: Browsers own file input values for security reasons, so file inputs are effectively uncontrolled.

realWorldUseCases: The docs include caret-jump bugs and controlled-to-uncontrolled warnings, so the real-world lens is strong.
actionableExamples: They include multiple concrete examples for controlled text inputs, checkboxes, and defaultValue-based uncontrolled inputs.
followUpConfusion: They directly answer tricky follow-ups like why value cannot switch from undefined to a string and why caret jumps on every keystroke.

realWorldUseCases: Add one production bug example around switching a field from uncontrolled to controlled after async data loads.
actionableExamples: Add a side-by-side defaultValue vs value example with reset and validation behavior.
followUpConfusion: Add explicit notes for file inputs, defaultValue, and the uncontrolled-to-controlled warning.

competitors.label: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled React Components?
competitors.selectionReason: This is a direct GreatFrontEnd quiz page on the exact question, so it is the strongest interview-prep comparator.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd answers the basic comparison well, but our page now goes further on the warning/debug path: async data causing uncontrolled-to-controlled flips, defaultValue versus value, reset behavior, and why file inputs stay DOM-owned.
theirStrengths: It is directly aligned with the exact interview question and includes controlled and uncontrolled examples.
theirGaps: It is lighter on production warnings like switching modes mid-lifecycle.

realWorldUseCases: A controlled input is owned by React state, which makes validation, derived UI, and reset flows predictable. An uncontrolled input is owned by the DOM, which is often simpler for one-off forms, file inputs, and low-friction submissions.
followUpConfusion: The warning A component is changing an uncontrolled input to be controlled usually means the same field started with DOM ownership and later received a React-controlled value.

realWorldUseCases: The quiz covers validation, complex interactions, and simple-form use cases, so the real-world criterion is close.
actionableExamples: It includes explicit controlled and uncontrolled form examples for the exact question.
followUpConfusion: It explains refs versus state clearly, though it is less explicit about React warnings and reset edge cases.

realWorldUseCases: Add one login-form versus file-input scenario to sharpen when each pattern is preferred.
actionableExamples: Add a defaultValue example and a controlled warning example, not just state and ref basics.
followUpConfusion: Add one sentence on why a field cannot switch from uncontrolled to controlled after mount.

competitors.label: Controlled vs Uncontrolled Components
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a strong focused reference because it covers real-world context, common pitfalls, and React 19 form-action tradeoffs.
competitors.winReason: Stanza still has the broader tutorial treatment, but our page is now better compressed around the production decision points interviewers usually probe: ownership, switching warnings, defaultValue, reset behavior, and file-input constraints.
theirStrengths: It is tightly focused on the topic and includes modern FormData and performance trade-offs.
theirGaps: It is tutorial-style and slightly broader than a compact interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: An uncontrolled input is owned by the DOM, which is often simpler for one-off forms, file inputs, and low-friction submissions.
followUpConfusion: Controlled inputs reset from React state; uncontrolled inputs reset through the DOM or form reset APIs.

realWorldUseCases: It names autocomplete, simple contact forms, and React 19 form actions as concrete use cases.
actionableExamples: It includes controlled and uncontrolled code examples, plus value versus defaultValue and FormData guidance.
followUpConfusion: It explicitly covers switching between controlled and uncontrolled and when uncontrolled becomes attractive again.

realWorldUseCases: Add one scenario about file inputs and server actions where uncontrolled is the right default.
actionableExamples: Add a ref-based uncontrolled example that uses FormData or a file input.
followUpConfusion: Add explicit coverage of defaultValue, read-only value props, and mixing controlled/uncontrolled modes.
